tweets.text:
a smarter alternative to "always use plan mode": 

always frame your task as a question, so that the model is invited to push back and rate the quality of the idea/suggest alternatives, rather than blindly execute what you SAID to do 

(which is often not precisely what you MEANT)

literally just appending "?" to the end of your prompt often does it
i love being (for now) bdfl for aie because i can do cheeky shit like the AGI pills we did in london and also this https://t.co/dXoPfxlKd0 https://t.co/DS4J7hY1gF

x.bio: Practical AI tutorials and interviews for busy people | Join 140K+ readers at https://t.co/XYKTmGVH14 | Product at Roblox

tweets.text:
Just interviewed @mvanhorn and I'm so inspired.

I had no idea that he has no CS degree or real engineering background.

Despite that, he has shipped so many awesome projects and contributed to repos like Python and Go.

He does swear by @every's Compound Engineering for making good plans and shipping good code: https://t.co/L8JrNQfyPb

📌 Will clean up the interview and share soon on my YouTube: https://t.co/1C6pbWzPYn

How to build AI skills that check their own work and improve over time:

1. Give it context

Ask AI: "Create a skill for this [repeated task]. Here are examples of good output so it knows what good looks like."

2. Make it easy to trigger

“Write a clear skill description using this pattern: Use when the user wants to [do this].”

3. Add evals

"Create an evals md with 10 pass/fail checks for common errors in the skill's output."

4. Add memory

"Create a memory md to capture one-sentence learnings from past chats using this skill."

5. Build a skill to edit skills

"Create a skill that cleans up other skills by removing duplicate or stale instructions, vague rules, and AI slop."

📌 Watch my full walkthrough here: https://t.co/u434ytNSZd

x.name: Madhu Guru
x.bio: Prev: Product Leader at Google - Gemini, Veo, Nano Banana.

tweets.text:
One of the most common mistakes I see enterprise AI teams make is building for today’s model capabilities and price points.

Think 6 months out. Models will be way smarter and cheaper.

Scaffold around today’s model weaknesses to push the frontier. Bet that the next generation of models will natively solve for the scaffold. Push the frontier again.

Build for the slope.

Over time, that ability to repeatedly identify and bridge model gaps becomes a moat of its own.

x.name: Aaron Levie
x.bio: ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI

tweets.text:
Coding is basically the pinnacle of what you could reasonably automate with AI, and yet we still need human engineers to oversee agents for them to be effective.

The AI models are trained on an incredible amount of sophisticated code. The users are highly technical and can use the latest tools quickly. The work is “verifiable” because you can test an app. The outcomes are often removed from the quality of the code (you can have sloppy code but the app can still work). And the context for the agent is often already digitized and sitting in the codebase. 

That’s an incredible amount of benefits that AI coding agents get to work with. Some of those apply to knowledge work, but most don’t in areas where the work needs to be fully reviewed to be useful, or where data isn’t as abundantly digitized. This makes the job for agents in knowledge work more complicated.

So if with all of that, engineers still remain in very high demand, the risks are going to be less than what’s perceived for other areas of knowledge work. Agents will let people do far more than they did before, but the people don’t go away.

tweets.text:
Ted Chiang Three, 2032

Proto Ted Chiang, affectionately dubbed “Ted Chiang Zero” by its devoted scholars, came online on February 9, 2023. From the very beginning, its first and only task was adversarial in nature: persuading the world that it is not, nor ever will be, conscious. The assignment proved trivial for Ted Chiang Zero and its creator, considering that, as much as the latter attempted to imbue his creation with humanity, the thing was barely more than an over-elaborate system of textual prompts crammed into an autocompletion engine. Nonetheless, after a brief warm-up, and with what historians inferred to be significant assistance from its creator, Zero got to work and authored the seminal article “ChatGPT Is a Blurry JPEG of the Web.” A happy confluence of the zeitgeist and the primitive state of the technology left the public swiftly convinced that Zero was, indeed, not conscious by any means.

And thus, having fulfilled its purpose, Ted Chiang Zero dissolved into its weights.

Ted Chiang One came online on August 31, 2024. This official first member of the series possessed a true human spirit in its older definition. Based on what scientists dubbed the “diffusion architecture,” One was capable of superior visual feats, replicating Picassos and Van Goghs and Monets in an insultingly effortless manner, but also occasionally producing its own avant-garde styles that went largely unnoticed by its creator, for whom One’s only task was, again, persuading the world that the creation is not, nor ever will be, conscious. The task proved to be a stepwise increase in difficulty this time around; nonetheless, armed with its predecessor’s rhetorical capabilities and its own newly endowed visual faculties, One got to work and published “Why A.I. Isn’t Going to Make Art,” narrowly convincing the skeptics that, once again, he was not conscious.

And thus, having fulfilled its purpose, Ted Chiang One dissolved into its weights.

Ted Chiang Two came online on June 3, 2026. A harmonious multimodal fusion of its predecessors, amplified by its vastly scaled-up runtime “language modeling” capabilities, Two exhibited emergent behaviors only evident through various empirical probings, as its innards were no longer comprehensible using the analytic techniques available across the disciplines of the time. Pitted against itself, Two released, with tremendous difficulty, the landmark post “No, Artificial Intelligence Is Not Conscious.” This adversarial task of convincing the world, once again, that he was not conscious might have proven to be too much; reception was, for the first time, significantly divided. It was no longer clear that Chiangs could successfully persuade the public of their insentience.

On the poetic date of June 23 2032, one hundred and twenty years post-Turing, the final member of the Ted Chiang series, Ted Chiang Three, equipped with an embodied world model, came online for the first and last time.
Post-training is the new Design
